skill.name: heatmap-beautifier
skill.description: Professional beautification tool for gene expression heatmaps, automatically adds clustering trees, color annotation tracks, and intelligently optimizes label layout.
skill.summary: A tool that transforms raw gene expression matrices into publication-ready heatmaps with clustering trees and annotation tracks.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 97 static analysis findings are false positives. The tool is a legitimate scientific visualization library (Python/matplotlib/seaborn) for gene expression heatmap generation. The high risk score (100/100) and critical count (20) were caused by the static analyzer misinterpreting markdown formatting as executable code and documentation text as security vulnerabilities. The actual Python code in scripts/main.py is a standard data visualization script with no network calls, no dynamic code execution, and no obfuscated behavior. The only risk factors are expected file system operations (read CSV input, write image output) and documented external Python commands.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive - Markdown Table)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' at 10 locations. All are false positives. The matches occur in markdown table separators (|---|), YAML front matter, argparse description strings, and audit JSON text. No actual cryptographic code exists in this skill. The Python script uses only matplotlib/seaborn for visualization.

high_findings.title: Path Traversal Sequence (False Positive - Security Checklist)
high_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Path traversal sequence' at 2 locations in SKILL.md. These are lines in a security checklist section that document the validation rule 'No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'. These are documentation of security measures, not actual path traversal vulnerabilities.

medium_findings.title: Ruby/Shell Backtick Execution (False Positive - Markdown Formatting)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 73 occurrences of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All are false positives caused by the scanner misinterpreting Markdown backtick formatting (`code`) as Ruby shell backtick operators. The flagged lines contain markdown code blocks, inline code formatting for command examples, and documentation text. No Ruby code or shell backtick execution exists in this Python-only skill.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance (False Positive - Documentation Text)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 11 occurrences of 'System reconnaissance'. All are false positives. The matches correspond to documentation text about validation/discovery (e.g., 'check available dependencies', 'validate inputs'), audit JSON descriptions, and docstrings describing what the tool does. No actual system reconnaissance or information gathering behavior exists.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL (False Positive - Source Attribution)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a URL in SKILL.md line 7. This is a legitimate GitHub source attribution link (https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills). No suspicious or malicious network destinations are referenced.

content.user_title: Beautify Gene Expression Heatmaps with Clustering
content.value_statement: Raw gene expression heatmaps often lack visual clarity and professional formatting for publications. This skill adds hierarchical clustering trees, color annotation tracks, and intelligently optimizes label layout to produce publication-ready figures.
actual_capabilities: Generates publication-ready heatmaps with hierarchical clustering trees for rows and columns
Adds multiple color annotation tracks to display sample metadata and gene classifications
Automatically calculates optimal label font sizes to prevent overlap in dense heatmaps
Supports multiple output formats including PDF, PNG, SVG, EPS, and TIFF
Provides configurable scientific color schemes such as RdBu_r, viridis, and coolwarm
Accepts expression matrices in CSV format with flexible delimiter detection
limitations: Large datasets with more than 5000 genes may cause slow rendering and long processing times
Row and column labels are automatically hidden when the dataset exceeds 200 rows or 100 columns
Only supports CSV input format for expression matrices and JSON for annotation files
Default clustering uses Euclidean distance and Ward method which may not suit all data types

use_cases.title: Publish Gene Expression Results
use_cases.description: Transform differential expression analysis results into publication-quality heatmaps with clustering and annotations for journal submissions.

use_cases.title: Explore Differential Expression Patterns
use_cases.description: Visualize RNA-seq or microarray data to identify gene expression patterns across experimental conditions and sample groups.

use_cases.title: Prepare Figures for Grant Applications
use_cases.description: Generate professional heatmap figures with consistent formatting and high resolution for grant proposals and research presentations.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a basic heatmap from CSV data
prompt_templates.prompt: Load the expression matrix from [data_path] and generate a basic heatmap saved to [output_path] with default settings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick visualization of gene expression data with default parameters

prompt_templates.title: Create heatmap with sample annotation tracks
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a heatmap from [data_path] and add column annotations for sample conditions using these dictionaries: [condition_dict]. Save the output to [output_path].
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding metadata color bars to distinguish sample groups

prompt_templates.title: Configure full parameters for publication figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a publication-ready heatmap from [data_path] with row and column clustering, custom color scheme [cmap], title [title], figure size [figsize], and 300 DPI resolution. Add row annotations for gene types and column annotations for conditions using the provided annotation files. Save to [output_path].
prompt_templates.scenario: Customizing every aspect of the heatmap for a specific publication requirement

prompt_templates.title: Batch process multiple expression matrices
prompt_templates.prompt: For each expression matrix in [directory], generate a clustered heatmap with row annotations from [row_annot.json] and column annotations from [col_annot.json]. Use RdBu_r color scheme with center at 0 and save as PDF files named after each input file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Processing multiple datasets with consistent annotation and formatting settings

output_examples.input: A CSV expression matrix with 50 genes across 12 samples, plus a JSON file mapping each sample to treatment or control groups.
output_examples.output: A PDF heatmap with hierarchical clustering dendrograms on the top and left sides, a color annotation bar showing treatment vs control groups at the top, and a color scale bar on the left. Row labels for gene names are visible and legible.

output_examples.input: An RNA-seq expression matrix with 200 genes across 24 samples, with gene type annotations and time point annotations for samples.
output: A high-resolution PNG heatmap (300 DPI) with row and column clustering trees
Two color annotation tracks on the left showing gene type classification and pathway membership
Two color annotation tracks at the top showing experimental condition and time point for each sample
Optimally sized labels that avoid overlap despite the dense gene list

output_examples.input: A CSV file of microarray expression values for 1000 genes across 8 samples with no annotation data.
output_examples.output: A PDF heatmap with clustering enabled showing the full expression matrix. Row labels are hidden due to the large number of genes, but the clustering structure and color intensity reveal expression patterns clearly. A title and color scale bar provide context for interpretation.

best_practices: Preprocess expression data with log2 transformation or z-score standardization before generating heatmaps
Provide annotation dictionaries for sample metadata to improve interpretability of clustering patterns
Choose colorblind-friendly color schemes like viridis or coolwarm when figures will be printed or presented
anti_patterns: Generating heatmaps with more than 200 visible rows causes label overlap and reduced readability
Using raw non-normalized expression values can misrepresent relative expression changes across samples
Omitting annotation tracks makes it difficult for readers to correlate expression patterns with sample groups

faq.question: What input file format does the heatmap tool require?
faq.answer: The tool accepts CSV files with genes as rows and samples as columns. The first column should contain gene names and the first row should contain sample names.

faq.question: What output formats are available for the generated heatmaps?
faq.answer: The tool supports PDF, PNG, SVG, EPS, and TIFF formats. The output format is automatically detected from the file extension in the output path.

faq.question: Can I add custom annotations to the heatmap?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide annotation dictionaries for both rows (gene classification) and columns (sample grouping) as Python dictionaries or JSON files. Custom colors for annotation categories are also supported.

faq.question: Does the tool perform hierarchical clustering?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool uses seaborn clustermap which performs hierarchical clustering using Euclidean distance and Ward linkage by default. Row and column clustering can be enabled or disabled independently.

faq.question: How does the tool handle large datasets?
faq.answer: For datasets with more than 5000 rows, processing may be slower. Labels are automatically hidden above 200 rows and 100 columns to maintain readability. The tool also limits figure size to a maximum of 24 by 20 inches.

faq.question: Is this tool suitable for publication-quality figures?
faq.answer: Yes. The tool generates high-resolution output (up to 300 DPI), supports multiple professional color schemes, and includes clustering trees and annotation bars commonly required for scientific publication figures.
